fragment_text: Creatine monohydrate is described as the form with the strongest ergogenic support for increasing high-intensity exercise capacity and lean mass during training.
dose_context: not specified in this extracted fragment
conditions_or_limits: Indirect support for strength; it should be combined with strength-specific meta-analyses.
annotation_notes: Cleaned: project-specific interpretation was removed and support strength for M01 was downgraded.

fragment_text: After a loading phase, performance in high-intensity and/or repeated exercise typically increases, making creatine especially relevant for power claims or short repeated efforts.
dose_context: not specified in this extracted fragment
conditions_or_limits: More useful for high-intensity and repeated efforts than for purely aerobic events.
annotation_notes: Cleaned: text made more faithful to the position stand and focused on high intensity.

fragment_text: Creatine is also associated with increases in lean mass during training, although that support is complementary and does not justify broad muscle-gain promises on its own.
dose_context: not specified in this extracted fragment
conditions_or_limits: For body composition, it should also be supported by specific meta-analyses; the effect does not justify inflated claims.
annotation_notes: Kept as partial support for lean_mass_gain.

fragment_text: Compared with placebo, creatine combined with resistance training significantly increased upper- and lower-body strength in adults under 50 years of age.
dose_context: high-dose tended to outperform lower-dose protocols; exact threshold not fixed in snippet
conditions_or_limits: Benefits appear larger in men than in women, and the finding is limited to adults under 50 years of age.
annotation_notes: Primary fragment for backed results on explicit strength claims.

fragment_text: The combination of creatine and resistance training produced a small increase in direct measures of regional muscle hypertrophy.
conditions_or_limits: The effect size is small; it supports the claim only partially, not strong muscle-gain promises.
annotation_notes: Very useful for distinguishing partially_backed from potentially_misleading in aggressive muscle claims.

fragment_text: Caffeine improves performance when consumed at doses of 3-6 mg/kg, and the minimum effective dose may be lower; this provides a reasonable basis for claims about reduced perceived fatigue or increased alertness, but not for exaggerated energy promises.
dose_context: 3-6 mg/kg body mass; minimal effective dose may be ~2 mg/kg
conditions_or_limits: Very high doses do not appear to add benefit and increase the risk of adverse effects.
annotation_notes: Cleaned: downgraded to moderate because 'energy' is an indirect commercial translation of the ergogenic/RPE effect.

fragment_text: Caffeine has consistently shown exercise-performance benefits, especially at moderate doses, and is relevant for endurance/performance claims.
conditions_or_limits: Individual response varies; tolerance and habitual intake can modulate the effect.
annotation_notes: No substantial changes; strong support for M05.

fragment_text: Acute caffeine intake can be used effectively as an ergogenic aid at moderate doses, producing a small but consistent improvement in endurance performance.
conditions_or_limits: The effect is small; it does not justify extreme claims.
annotation_notes: Cleaned: text made closer to the meta-analysis.

fragment_text: Caffeine reduces the subjective rating of effort during exercise, providing a specific basis for claims about reduced perceived fatigue.
dose_context: not standardized in this extracted fragment
conditions_or_limits: Perceptual outcome; it should not be extrapolated to a generic promise of extreme energy.
annotation_notes: Kept; it is a good specific complement for M04.

fragment_text: The review of whey for recovery of muscle function after resistance exercise found mixed results: only some studies reported benefits, and the literature is described as equivocal.
conditions_or_limits: Whey-specific evidence for recovery is not uniform; it does not support strong accelerated-recovery claims.
annotation_notes: Major correction: the previous version overstated the support. It was revised to partial-weak support.

fragment_text: Peri-exercise protein may help preserve maximal strength and reduce creatine kinase after resistance exercise, but it showed no clear effect on muscle soreness.
conditions_or_limits: Indirect evidence for whey because the document addresses supplemental protein in general, not whey in isolation.
annotation_notes: Kept as partial and indirect support for M07.

fragment_text: Protein supplementation significantly improved changes in strength and muscle size during prolonged resistance training in healthy adults.
dose_context: benefit plateau beyond ~1.6 g/kg/day total protein intake
conditions_or_limits: Indirect evidence for whey: the meta-analysis is about supplemental protein in general, not specifically whey.
retrieval_keywords: protein supplementation|lean body mass|muscle size|resistance training
annotation_notes: Cleaned: downgraded from yes/strong to partial/moderate because whey specificity is lacking.

fragment_text: Above an approximate total daily intake of 1.6 g/kg/day, protein supplementation provided no additional benefits for fat-free mass or strength during resistance training.
conditions_or_limits: Limitation fragment; useful for constraining claims such as more protein always means more muscle.
annotation_notes: Kept as a very useful limitation fragment.

fragment_text: Beta-alanine supplementation showed a significant overall effect, with the largest effects appearing in exercises lasting 0.5 to 10 minutes, especially on exercise capacity rather than broad performance.
dose_context: not specified in extracted meta-analysis snippet
conditions_or_limits: The evidence is stronger for exercise capacity than for any generic performance claim.
annotation_notes: Cleaned: the time window and the capacity vs performance distinction are made explicit.

fragment_text: The ISSN position stand states that daily beta-alanine supplementation of 4 to 6 g for at least 2 to 4 weeks can improve exercise performance and act as an ergogenic aid.
conditions_or_limits: More relevant to intense efforts; it is not universal support for every modality.
annotation_notes: Kept; a good fragment for rules involving dose and duration.
